title: Paris — a night at the opera
matchup: Paul Morphy vs Duke of Brunswick & Count Isouard
event: Paris — a night at the opera
blurb: The most famous teaching game ever played. Morphy, the best player of his day, dispatched two amateurs consulting together — in a private box, mid-opera. A clinic in fast development and the attack it buys.
comments: Morphy plays the two amateurs while the opera carries on around them.
The Philidor Defence — safe, but it hems in Black's own bishop.
Pinning the knight. This bishop will end up costing Black dearly.
Forced: on 4...dxe5 5. Qxd8+ Kxd8 Black loses the right to castle.
The bishop aims straight at f7 — the softest square in Black's camp.
A double attack: the queen hits b7 and, with the bishop, f7.
Pure Morphy. He passes up the free b7-pawn to keep every piece developing with threats.
The first sacrifice — lines and time are worth more than a knight.
Now every white piece bears down on the pinned knight.
He castles with tempo: the rook lands on the d-file, adding to the pin.
Tearing away the last defender.
The second rook joins. Black is completely tied up and can barely move.
The finishing combination begins — a queen sacrifice to deflect the knight.
Checkmate. Down a queen and more, Morphy mates because every piece was working. The whole game is one long argument for rapid development.

title: London — the Immortal Game
blurb: Played casually between rounds of the first international tournament, this is the game that named a genre. Anderssen gives up a bishop, both rooks and the queen — and mates with the three minor pieces he has left.
comments: The King's Gambit: White offers a pawn to blast open the centre before Black is ready.
White forfeits castling but wins time chasing the loose queen.
Developing with tempo — the knight hits the queen.
Guarding f4, but the knight drifts to the edge and does nothing there.
A fearless leap into Black's position.
Trapping the h5-knight in the middle of the fight.
A quiet rook move — and a calm sacrifice of the b5-bishop.
Threatening Bxf4, which would trap the queen.
Black is pushed all the way back to the starting rank.
Both sides ignore ordinary development to fight over the initiative.
With tempo — attacking the queen again.
Black grabs a rook's worth of pawns, and walks into immortality.
The stunning point: Anderssen offers a whole rook to jam Black's pieces in each other's way.
Slamming the door on the queen so it can never come back to defend.
Trying, too late, to cover the dark squares around the king.
A second queen offer — dragging the last defender off f6.
Checkmate. Two knights and a bishop finish the job, having sacrificed a bishop, two rooks and the queen to get there.

title: Berlin — the Evergreen Game
blurb: Wilhelm Steinitz called it 'evergreen in the laurel crown' of Anderssen. Another Evans Gambit, another storm of sacrifices — capped by one of the prettiest queen offers on record.
comments: The Evans Gambit: a pawn thrown in to seize tempo and the centre.
Black hands the pawn back to slow White's build-up.
The bishop stops Black from castling to safety.
A shot to rip open the g-file.
Both kings are in the open now — it is a straight race to mate.
The move that made the game evergreen: Anderssen ignores his own hanging bishop and brings the last piece in.
The combination breaks. Everything is sacrificed with a purpose.
A queen sacrifice to strip the king bare.
The double-check idea hauls the king into a net it can't escape.
Checkmate. A finish worth memorising just for its beauty.

title: New York — the Game of the Century
matchup: Donald Byrne vs Robert James Fischer
event: New York — the Game of the Century
blurb: A 13-year-old Bobby Fischer against a leading American master. On move 17 Fischer gives up his queen — and the storm of minor pieces that follows is so precise it earned the game its grand nickname.
comments: Fischer ignores the loss of a tempo to develop with threats.
A slip — the bishop should have stayed home.
The move that lights the fuse, hitting the queen and the c3-knight at once.
Winning back the piece with interest and cracking White's centre open.
The immortal move: Fischer offers his queen, and it must not be taken.
Byrne accepts.
Now the minor pieces hunt the white king with check after check.
The smoke clears: Fischer has two rooks, two bishops and a knight for the queen — a winning haul.
Checkmate, delivered by a coordinated net of minor pieces. Astonishing from a boy of thirteen.

title: Wijk aan Zee — Kasparov's Immortal
blurb: Widely called the greatest game of the modern era. Kasparov sacrifices a rook to drag Topalov's king on a march from b8 all the way across the board — and calculates the whole hunt to mate.
comments: A Pirc where both sides castle into opposite corners — the recipe for a race.
The tension is unbearable; Kasparov now unleashes it.
The first blow — the rook is offered to blow open Black's king.
The king is forced out into the open, and the hunt begins.
Topalov's king has walked from b8 to d1 under a hail of checks.
Black resigned. The king hunt Kasparov saw from move 24 comes home a piece up and winning. A masterpiece of calculation.
